docs.title: goboscript
docs.text:
Installation and documentation

goboscript is a text-based programming language which compiles to Scratch. It allows you to write Scratch projects in text, and compile it into a .sb3 file - which can be opened in the Scratch editor, TurboWarp or be uploaded to the Scratch website.

goboscript allows you to create advanced Scratch projects with ease, you can use any text editor, use a version control system such as git. You can refactor your code using search and replace. Text code can be copy pasted, which allows you to easily reuse code or share it with others. goboscript syntax is concise and easy to read.

goboscript allows you to integrate external tooling and workflows, such as using a script to generate costumes for a text rendering engine. Or loading in images into lists.

goboscript has a powerful macro system - similar to Rust's macro system. This allows you to write macros to generate code.

goboscript is more than just an 1:1 mapping of Scratch blocks to text, it also has additional features like local variables for procedures (custom blocks).

goboscript also performs optimizations, detects problems and unused code.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
goboscript welcomes contributions in the form of Pull Requests.

goboscript is written in Rust. You'll need to install the Rust toolchain for development.

[!NOTE] To install goboscript, follow instructions at aspizu.github.io/goboscript. These instructions are for people who want to develop goboscript itself.

git clone https://github.com/aspizu/goboscript
cd goboscript

docs.title: Development
docs.text:
After cloning the repository, run goboscript locally from the repository root with:

cargo run -- build -i your_project/

But, to make development easier, and to validate the generated Scratch project - use the tools/run script:

tools/run compile

This assumes that you have set-up a testing project at playground/. It will compile the project, validate it using the schemas from scratch-parser. If the validation fails, Scratch will refuse to load the project. To further debug the project, the generated project.json file is extracted from the .sb3 file in the playground/ directory.

Lets say that you modified the generated project in the Scratch editor or Turbowarp, and you want to look at the project.json. You can extract it with:

tools/run uncompile

Lets say that goboscript produced a broken project, and you are able to fix it by hand - by modifying the project.json. You can add back the project.json to the .sb3 file with:

tools/run patch

If you want to validate some .sb3 file, use:

tools/run check path/to/project.sb3

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: goboscript uses a goboscript.toml configuration file to store project-specific configuration.

docs.title: Turbowarp options
docs.text: goboscript can generate a turbowarp configuration comment inside the Stage. https://docs.turbowarp.org/advanced-settings

docs.title: Frame Rate
docs.text: frame_rate = 60

docs.title: Default
docs.text: max_clones = 300

docs.title: Infinite Clones
docs.text: max_clones = inf

docs.title: No Miscellaneous Limits
docs.text: no_miscellaneous_limits = true

docs.title: No Sprite Fencing
docs.text: no_sprite_fencing = true

docs.title: Frame Interpolation
docs.text: frame_interpolation = true

docs.title: High Quality Pen
docs.text: high_quality_pen = true

docs.title: Stage Width & Height
docs.text:
stage_width = 640
stage_height = 480

docs.title: Install from binaries
docs.text:
Pre-built binaries are available for Windows, macOS, and Linux.

Download the latest release from https://github.com/aspizu/goboscript/releases, and extract the archive to a folder in your PATH.

On Windows, you can copy the goboscript.exe file to C:\Windows\System32 to make it available from the command line.

docs.title: Install from source
docs.text:
This installs the latest bleeding-edge version from the git repository. You will need git, and the rust toolchain installed.

git clone https://github.com/aspizu/goboscript
cd goboscript
cargo install --path .

To update the installation:

cd goboscript
git pull
cargo install --path .

docs.title: Install from source (using cargo)
docs.text:
This installs the latest bleeding-edge version from the git repository with a single command.

cargo install --git https://github.com/aspizu/goboscript

To update the installation:

cargo install --git https://github.com/aspizu/goboscript --force

docs.title: Install from crates.io
docs.text:
This installs the latest stable version from crates.io with a single command.

cargo install goboscript

To update the installation:

cargo install goboscript --force

docs.title: Standard Library
docs.text: The standard library is a collection of useful procedures, functions and macros that can be used in any goboscript project.

docs.title: Standard library headers
docs.text:
Include a header using the %include directive.

%include std/math

goboscript's dead code elimination will remove any unused procedures and functions from the compiled project.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
There is no hand-written documentation currently, so refer to AI-generated documentation.

https://deepwiki.com/goboscript/std

docs.title: Syntax Highlighting
docs.text:
Copy the /editors/notepad++/goboscript.udl.xml file to the Notepad++ user defined language directory.

Language > User Defined Language > Open User Defined Language Folder...

docs.title: Sublime Text
docs.text: The Sublime Text extension provides syntax highlighting.

docs.title: Install from source
docs.text: in Sublime Text, click Preferences > Browse Packages... to open up the Packages folder, and then copy editors/sublime/goboscript.sublime-syntax into the Packages folder

docs.title: Instant Project Reload
docs.text:
This will add a keyboard shortcut to instantly reload the project in TurboWarp Desktop using Ctrl+B

Open the user data folder using: Settings > Desktop Settings > Open User Data

Create a file called userscript.js and add the following code:

window.addEventListener('keyup', async (event) => {
  if (!(event.key === 'b' && event.ctrlKey)) return
  event.preventDefault()
  const id = await EditorPreload.getInitialFile()
  if (id === null) return
  const file = await EditorPreload.getFile(id)
  await vm.loadProject(file.data)
})

Restart TurboWarp Desktop including all open windows.

Thanks to GarboMuffin for this code.

docs.title: Visual Studio Code
docs.text: The VSCode extension provides code snippets and syntax highlighting.

docs.title: Install from source
docs.text:
You will need npm installed.

# Inside the root of the goboscript git repository:
cd editors/code
npm install
npm run package

This will output a goboscript.vsix file in the editors/code directory. You can install it by pressing Ctrl+Shift+P and typing Extensions: Install from VSIX....

docs.title: Configure Build Task
docs.text:
You can configure the goboscript build task to get diagnostics in vscode.

Add to .vscode/tasks.json:

{
  "version": "2.0.0",
  "tasks": [
    {
      "type": "goboscript-build",
      "problemMatcher": ["$rustc"],
      "group": {
        "kind": "build",
        "isDefault": true
      },
      "label": "Build .sb3"
    }
  ]
}

Either press Ctrl+Shift+B or run Tasks: Run Task and select Build .sb3.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: The goboscript compiler is a command-line program. You can create a new project using the new command. (run goboscript new --help for more information)

docs.title: Create a new project
docs.text:
Create a new folder, and make sure that your working-directory is set to that folder.

goboscript new

This will create a new project with the following structure:

.
├── blank.svg
├── main.gs
├── stage.gs
└── goboscript.toml

Depending on what options you chose, it might create a goboscript.toml configuration file.

Each .gs file holds the code for a sprite, the name of the sprite is the name of the file without the .gs extension.

stage.gs holds the code for the Stage. Scratch does not allow you to name a sprite Stage, so creating a file with the name Stage.gs is invalid. As goboscript uses stage.gs for the Stage, you also cannot name a sprite stage (in lowercase).

blank.svg is a blank costume. You can see that both the main sprite and the Stage have the line:

costumes "blank.svg";

This is used to add a costume to a sprite (or the Stage), see language/costumes for more information.

docs.title: Compile the project
docs.text:
To compile the project, run the following command:

goboscript build

This will compile the project into a .sb3 file. The .sb3 file will be placed in the project directory. It will have the same name as the project directory.

Run goboscript build --help for more information.

docs.title: Basic Examples
docs.text:
Let's start with simple examples of Goboscript syntax.

# This is a single-line comment

# Numbers
var integer = 42;        # Integer
var float_num = 3.14;    # Float
var binary = 0b1010;     # Binary (value: 10)
var hex = 0xFF;          # Hexadecimal (value: 255)
var octal = 0o777;       # Octal (value: 511)

# Strings
var greeting = "Hello, World!";
var escaped = "Quotes: \"example\"";
var unicode = "\u1234";  # Unicode character

# Booleans
var flag = true;         # Will be compiled as 1
var not_flag = false;    # Will be compiled as 0

docs.title: Variables and Operators
docs.text:
# Variable declaration and assignment
x = 10;
y = 20;
sum = x + y;         # Addition: 30
diff = x - y;        # Subtraction: -10
product = x * y;     # Multiplication: 200
quotient = y / x;    # Division: 2
floor_div = y // x;  # Floor division: 2
remainder = y % x;   # Modulo: 0
text = "Hello" & " World"; # String concatenation: "Hello World"

# Compound assignment
x += 5;                  # x = x + 5
y -= 3;                  # y = y - 3
product *= 2;            # product = product * 2
quotient /= 4;           # quotient = quotient / 4
text &= "!";             # text = text & "!"

# Comparison operators
is_equal = x == y;   # Equal to
not_equal = x != y;  # Not equal to
greater = x > y;     # Greater than
less = x < y;        # Less than
greater_equal = x >= y; # Greater than or equal to
less_equal = x <= y;    # Less than or equal to

# Logical operators
both = x > 0 and y > 0;  # Logical AND
either = x > 0 or y < 0; # Logical OR
inverse = not x > 0;     # Logical NOT

docs.title: Control Flow
docs.text:
condition = true;
x = 10;

# Simple if statement
if x > 5 {
    say "x is greater than 5";
}

# If-else statement
if x % 2 == 0 {
    say "x is even";
} else {
    say "x is odd";
}

# If-elif-else statement
if x < 0 {
    say "x is negative";
} elif x == 0 {
    say "x is zero";
} else {
    say "x is positive";
}

# Boolean coercion
if timer() {  # Equivalent to: if timer() == 1
    say "Timer is active";
}

docs.title: Loops
docs.text:
# Repeat loop (fixed number of iterations)
repeat 5 {
    say "Repeated message";
}

# Repeat with counter
var i = 1;
repeat 10 {
    say "Iteration " & i;
    i++;
}

# Until loop (continues until condition is true)
var counter = 0;
until counter > 5 {
    say "Counter: " & counter;
    counter++;
}

# Forever loop (infinite loop)
forever {
    say "Press stop to exit";
    if key space pressed? {
        stop_this_script;
    }
}

docs.title: Procedures
docs.text:
# Define a procedure
proc greet_user {
    say "Hello, user!";
}

# Procedure with parameters
proc personalized_greeting name {
    say "Hello, " & $name & "!";
}

# Procedure with local variables
proc calculate_sum a, b {
    local result = $a + $b;
    say "Sum: " & result;
}

onflag {
    # Call procedures
    greet_user;
    personalized_greeting "John";
    calculate_sum 5, 10;
}

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
Functions are similar to procedures but return values.

# Define a function
func add(x, y) {
    return $x + $y;
}

# Function with type
func create_greeting(name) {
    return "Hello, " & $name & "!";
}

onflag {
    message = create_greeting("World");
    say message;  # Outputs: "Hello, World!"
}

docs.title: Lists
docs.text:
# Declare an empty list
list my_list;

# Add items to a list
add "apple" to my_list;
add "banana" to my_list;
add "cherry" to my_list;

# Access list items (1-indexed)
first_item = my_list[1];  # "apple"

# Replace items
my_list[2] = "blueberry";     # Replace "banana" with "blueberry"

# Insert at position
insert "apricot" at my_list[1]; # Insert at the beginning

# Delete an item
delete my_list[3];

# Check length
size = length my_list;

# Check if an item is in the list
if "cherry" in my_list {
    say "Found cherry!";
}

# Get random item
random_fruit = my_list["random"];

# Clear the list
delete my_list;

# Load list from file
list data = file ```data.txt```;

docs.title: Structures and Enums
docs.text:
# Define a struct
struct Point {
    x,
    y
}

# Create a struct instance
Point p = Point { x: 10, y: 20 };

# Access struct fields
say "Coordinates: " & p.x & ", " & p.y;

# Define an enum
enum Direction {
    North,  # 0
    East,   # 1
    South,  # 2
    West    # 3
}

# Use enum values
current_direction = Direction.North;
if current_direction == Direction.North {
    say "Heading north";
}

# Enum with explicit values
enum Color {
    Red = "red",
    Green = "green",
    Blue = "blue"
}

say "Selected color: " & Color.Red;

docs.title: Blocks
docs.text:
The general syntax for blocks is:

block_name arg1, arg2;

docs.title: repeat n times
docs.text:
repeat n {
    # code
}

docs.title: repeat until condition
docs.text:
until condition == true {
    # code
}

docs.title: forever loop
docs.text:
forever {
    # code
}

docs.title: if
docs.text:
if condition {
    # code
}

docs.title: if else
docs.text:
if condition {
    # code
}
else {
    # code
}

docs.title: if elif
docs.text:
if condition {
    # code
}
elif condition {
    # code
}

docs.title: Costumes
docs.text:
You can add costumes to a sprite by specifying their file paths relative to the project directory.

costumes "path/to/costume.svg";

docs.title: Listing Multiple Costumes
docs.text:
To add multiple costumes, separate their file paths with commas. Costumes will appear in the order you list them in the costumes statement.

costumes "path/to/costume1.svg", "path/to/costume2.svg";

Each costume's name is taken from the file name without its extension.

docs.title: Renaming Costumes
docs.text:
You can rename a costume using the as keyword.

costumes "path/to/costume.svg" as "new name";

docs.title: Using Wildcards (Globs)
docs.text:
You can use wildcards to include multiple costumes, such as all .svg files in a directory. Use the * wildcard for this.

costumes "path/to/costumes/*.svg";

Costumes added this way are sorted alphabetically.

docs.title: Generating costumes for text engines and case detection
docs.text:
Scratch compares strings case-insensitively. Switching costumes is, however, case-sensitive. This can be utilized to detect the case of a character by first switching to the costume named by the character, then using the costume number to detect the case.

For example, if you have a costume named "A" at position 1, and a costume named "a" at position 2, you can use the following code to detect the case of a character:

switch_costume char;
if costume_number() == 1 {
    say "upper case A";
} else {
    say "lower case a";
}

Its useful to have one costume for each printable character in the ASCII set. This will allow you to get the ASCII value of any printable character.

Writing costumes "blank.svg" as "A", "blank.svg" as "B" ... for each printable character is a pain.

goboscript provides a special declaration for generating such costumes automatically.

costumes "blank.svg" as "@ascii/PREFIX";

This will generate costumes for all printable characters in the ASCII set, with the prefix "PREFIX". For example, if the prefix is "A", the costumes will be named "A0", "A1", etc.

If you do not wish to have a prefix, leave it blank. (i.e. @ascii/)

Given that these are placed at the beginning of the costumes list, you can get the ASCII value of a character by adding 31 to the costume number.

docs.title: Custom Blocks
docs.text: Custom blocks, also known as procedures can take input arguments, but unlike functions, they do not return values.

docs.title: Declaring a Custom Block
docs.text:
Use the proc keyword to define a custom block. List argument names separated by commas.

proc my_procedure arg1, arg2 {
    say $arg1;
    say $arg2;
}

Use the nowarp keyword before proc to make the custom block run without screen refresh unchecked.

nowarp proc my_procedure arg1, arg2 {
    say $arg1;
    say $arg2;
}

docs.title: Struct-Typed Arguments
docs.text:
You can take in struct values by specifying the type name before the argument name.

proc process_item Item item_data {
    say $item_data.name;
}

docs.title: Default Argument Values
docs.text:
Just like functions, procedures support default argument values. This allows a caller to skip certain arguments when calling the block.

proc greet name = "world" {
    say "Hello, " & $name & "!";
}
  • greet → says "Hello, world!"
  • greet "aspizu" → says "Hello, aspizu!"

docs.title: Keyword Arguments
docs.text:
Procedures can also be called using keyword arguments, specifying each parameter by name. This improves readability, especially when not all parameters are passed or when calling with many arguments.

proc introduce name, title = "developer", location = "unknown" {
    say $name & " is a " & $title & " from " & $location;
}

Call it using keyword arguments:

introduce name: "aspizu", location: "India";
# Output: "aspizu is a developer from India"

Keyword arguments can be used in any order, as long as required arguments are provided:

introduce location: "Berlin", name: "Kai";
# Output: "Kai is a developer from Berlin"

docs.title: Calling Custom Blocks
docs.text:
Call a procedure using positional or keyword arguments:

# Positional
my_procedure "hello", 3;

# Keyword
my_procedure arg2: 3, arg1: "hello";

Use $argname inside the block to access the arguments.

docs.title: Enums
docs.text:
Enum variants will automatically be assigned a value starting from 0.

enum Direction {
    North, #    0
    East,  #    1
    South, #    2
    West   #    3
}

An explicit value can be given to an enum variant.

enum Direction {
    North = "North",
    East = "East",
    South = "South",
    West = "West"
}

Explicit values and implicit values can be mixed.

enum Direction {
    A = "A",
    B, #       0
    C = "C",
    D, #       1
    E = 2,
    F, #       3

}

## Get enum value

```goboscript
say Direction.North;

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
Functions are reusable procedures (custom blocks) that can return values, including primitives or structs. Functions always run in Run without screen refresh mode and must only be called from other Run without screen refresh procedures or functions to prevent undefined behavior.

Each function must end with a return statement. Using stop_this_script inside a function is undefined behavior.

docs.title: Declaring a Function
docs.text:
Use the func keyword to define a function. Optionally, include a return type for functions that return a struct.

func my_function(x, y) {
    return $x + $y;
}

func my_function(x, y) MyStruct {
    return MyStruct { ... };
}

docs.title: Default Argument Values
docs.text:
Function parameters can have default values, allowing callers to omit them:

func greet(name = "world") {
    return "Hello, " & $name & "!";
}
  • greet() returns "Hello, world!"
  • greet("aspizu") returns "Hello, aspizu!"

docs.title: Calling a Function
docs.text:
Functions are called by name with argument values:

say my_function(1, 2);

docs.title: Keyword Arguments
docs.text:
You can also call functions using keyword arguments, which specify parameter names explicitly. This is useful when using default arguments or calling functions with many parameters:

greet(name: "aspizu")

This behaves the same as greet("aspizu"), but makes the call more readable—especially when multiple parameters are involved:

func introduce(name, title = "developer", location = "unknown") {
    return $name & " is a " & $title & " from " & $location;
}

Call it with keyword arguments:

introduce(name: "aspizu", location: "India")
# Equivalent to: introduce("aspizu", "developer", "India")

Note

Keyword arguments can be used in any order, as long as the required parameters are provided:

introduce(location: "Berlin", name: "Kai");
# Still valid

docs.title: When Green Flag Clicked
docs.text:
onflag {
    # code
}

docs.title: When Key Pressed
docs.text:
onkey "enter" {
   # code 
}

docs.title: When This Sprite Clicked
docs.text:
onclick {
    # code
}

docs.title: When Backdrop Switches To
docs.text:
onbackdrop "backdrop" {
    # code
}

docs.title: When Loudness >
docs.text:
onloudness > 100 {
    # code
}

docs.title: When Timer >
docs.text:
ontimer > 100 {
    # code
}

docs.title: When I Start As A Clone
docs.text:
onclone {
    # code
}

docs.title: When I Receive Message
docs.text:
on "message name" {
    # code
}

docs.title: Lists
docs.text: The same rules apply for lists as for variables regarding for all sprites and for this sprite only.

docs.title: Declaration
docs.text:
list list_name;

list type_name list_name;

docs.title: Add item to list
docs.text: add value to list_name;

docs.title: Delete item from list at index
docs.text: delete list_name[index];

docs.title: Delete all items from list
docs.text: delete list_name;

docs.title: Insert item at index in list
docs.text: insert value at list_name[index];

docs.title: Replace item at index in list
docs.text: list_name[index] = value;

docs.title: Get item at index in list
docs.text: value = list_name[index];

docs.title: Get index of item in list
docs.text: index in item

docs.title: Get length of list
docs.text: len = length list_name;

docs.title: Check if item is in list
docs.text:
if value in list_name {
    ...
}

docs.title: Show/Hide List Monitor
docs.text:
show list_name;

hide list_name;

docs.title: Get random/last item in list
docs.text:
value = list_name["random"];

value = list_name["last"];

docs.title: Compound Assignment
docs.text: Operator Implementation list_name[index]++; list_name[index]--; list_name[index] += y; list_name[index] -= y; list_name[index] *= y; list_name[index] /= y; list_name[index] //= y; list_name[index] %= y; list_name[index] &= y;

docs.title: List Data
docs.text: Initial data for lists can be stored inside the project. This behaves the same way loading text files into lists works in the Scratch editor. In addition to loading text files, you can also load data from various scripts and commands. This is useful for creating look-up tables or loading data from images or videos.

docs.title: Loading data from text files
docs.text:
Each line in the text file will be added to the list as a separate item.

list list_name = file ```path/to/file.txt```;

docs.title: Loading data from bash script
docs.text:
The bash script enclosed in triple-backticks will be executed, and the standard output will be stored in the list, one item per line. The working directory will be set to the project directory.

list list_name = ```cat path/to/file.txt```;

docs.title: Loading data from any other program
docs.text:
The name of the program may be specified before the triple-backticks. This program will be executed with the standard input set to the script enclosed in the triple-backticks. The standard output will be stored in the list, one item per line. The working directory will be set to the project directory.

Any program that accepts input from stdin and outputs to stdout can be used.

For example, to load data from a python script:

list list_name = python ```
import random
for _ in range(5):
    print(random.randint(-5, 5))
```;

Tip

If your script takes a long time to run, you can use bash to cache the output of the script.

Let's say that your script converts a file DEPENDENCY.txt. You wish to only re-run the script if the file DEPENDENCY.txt has changed. We can use stat to get the last modification time of the file.

TIME=$(stat -c %Y DEPENDENCY.txt)
if [ $TIME -eq $(< DEPENDENCY.time)]; then
    cat DEPENDENCY.cached
    exit
fi
echo $TIME > DEPENDENCY.time
python convert_file.py DEPENDENCY.txt | tee DEPENDENCY.cached

docs.title: Struct List Data
docs.text:
If the list's type is a struct, each field will be filled with the value of the corresponding line in the data.

Example:

struct vec3d { x, y, z };
list vec3d points = file ```file.txt```;

contents of file.txt: 

10
20
30
40
50
60

resulting in the following list:

[
    vec3d {
        x: 10,
        y: 20,
        z: 30
    },
    vec3d {
        x: 40,
        y: 50,
        z: 60
    }
]

docs.title: Macros
docs.text:
goboscript has a C-like preprocessor. This allows you to define macros and include files.

Note

The preprocessor directives start with a % character. The % character must always appear at the start of a line. There cannot be any indentation before the % character.

docs.title: Include
docs.text:
Include the contents of a file.

%include path/to/file.gs

The .gs extension is optional. If not specified (recommended), the file extension will be added automatically.

If the include path is a directory, the file inside the directory with the same name as the directory will be included.

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Define a macro. That identifier will be substituted with the subsequent text.

%define macro_name replacement text

docs.title: Define with arguments
docs.text:
Define a macro with arguments. The arguments will be substituted with the tokens from the callsite.

%define macro_name(arg1, arg2) replacement text

You can use a backslash \ at the end of a line to continue the replacement text onto the next line:

%define long_macro this is a very long \
                   replacement text that spans \
                   multiple lines

docs.title: Remove a macro definition
docs.text: %undef macro_name

docs.title: Conditional compilation
docs.text:
%if macro_name
    code
%endif

%if not macro_name
    code
%endif

docs.title: Concatenate Tokens
docs.text: CONCAT(prefix, suffix) # becomes prefixsuffix

docs.title: Unary Operators
docs.text: Operator Implementation not x == true length x round x abs x floor x ceil x sqrt x sin x cos x tan x asin x acos x atan x ln x log x antiln x antilog x

docs.title: Binary Operators
docs.text: Operator Implementation x + y x - y x * y x / y x // y x % y x & y x == y x != y x < y x <= y x > y x >= y x == true and y == true x == true or y == true x in y x not in y x[y]

docs.title: Random
docs.text: random(x, y)

docs.title: Reporters
docs.text:
The general syntax for reporters is:

reporter_name(arg1, arg2)

docs.title: Sounds
docs.text:
Note

Scratch only supports MP3 and WAV files. Other formats will (as of now) not raise a warning or error, and the project will refuse to load in Scratch.

You can add sounds to a sprite by specifying their file paths relative to the project directory.

sounds "path/to/sound.mp3";

docs.title: Listing Multiple Sounds
docs.text:
To add multiple sounds, separate their file paths with commas. Sounds will appear in the order you list them in the sounds statement.

sounds "path/to/sound1.mp3", "path/to/sound2.wav";

Each sound's name is taken from the file name without its extension.

docs.title: Renaming Sounds
docs.text:
You can rename a sound using the as keyword.

sounds "path/to/sound.mp3" as "new name";

docs.title: Using Wildcards (Globs)
docs.text:
You can use wildcards to include multiple sounds, such as all .mp3 files in a directory. Use the * wildcard for this.

sounds "path/to/sounds/*.mp3";

Sounds added this way are sorted alphabetically.

docs.title: Sprites
docs.text: A sprite is a .gs file in the root of the project directory, all other .gs files (which are inside sub-directories of the project directory, or outside of the project directory, are not sprites.) are header files.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
Sprite properties can be set using statements similar to blocks which set those properties at runtime.

Note

These statements are top-level, outside any declaration block.

docs.title: Sprite default X position
docs.text: set_x 100;

docs.title: Sprite default Y position
docs.text: set_y 100;

docs.title: Sprite default size
docs.text: set_size 100;

docs.title: Sprite default direction
docs.text: point_in_direction 100;

docs.title: Sprite default volume
docs.text: set_volume 100;

docs.title: Set sprite default visibility to hidden
docs.text:
Hide sprite by default.

hide;

docs.title: Set Sprite default rotation style to left-right
docs.text: set_rotation_style_left_right;

docs.title: Set Sprite default rotation style to all around
docs.text: set_rotation_style_all_around;

docs.title: Set Sprite default rotation style to don't rotate
docs.text: set_rotation_style_do_not_rotate;

docs.title: Set Sprite layer order
docs.text:
Set the layer order of the sprite. The layer order is the order in which the sprites are drawn, and scripts are executed.

set_layer_order 100;

docs.title: Structs
docs.text: Structs are a way to group related variables or lists together.

docs.title: Declaration
docs.text:
struct my_struct {
    x,
    y,
    z
}

docs.title: Default values
docs.text:
Struct fields can have default values.

struct my_struct {
    field = "default_value"
}

my_struct v = my_struct {};
# -> v.field == "default_value"

docs.title: Declaring a struct variable
docs.text: my_struct my_variable = value;

docs.title: Declaring a struct list
docs.text: list my_struct my_list;

docs.title: Accessing a struct variable field
docs.text: my_variable.my_field

docs.title: Accessing a struct argument field
docs.text: $my_argument.my_field

docs.title: Accessing a struct list field
docs.text: my_list[index].my_field

docs.title: Struct literals
docs.text:
Struct literals are a way to create structs.

my_struct my_variable = my_struct {
    x: 10,
    y: 20,
    z: 30
};

docs.title: Passing structs
docs.text:
You can pass structs to procedures via arguments or variables.

list my_struct my_list;

proc my_procedure my_struct arg {
    # code
}

onflag {
    my_procedure my_struct {
        x: 10,
        y: 20,
        z: 30
    };
    my_procedure my_list[1];
}

onflag {
    my_struct foo = my_struct {
        x: 10,
        y: 20,
        z: 30
    };
    my_struct bar = foo;
}

docs.title: Variables
docs.text:
During struct variable assignment, fields are assigned sequentially. Consequently, if a field references another field of the same struct during assignment, it will use the updated value of previously assigned fields rather than their original values.

For example:

pair = Pair { left: 100, right: 200 };
pair = Pair { left: pair.left + 100, right: pair.left };
# Result: pair = Pair { left: 200, right: 200 };

In this case, the pair.left field is assigned first with the value 200, and subsequently pair.right references the updated value of pair.left.

This behavior can be mitigated by reordering the fields in the struct literal:

pair = Pair { right: pair.left, left: pair.left + 100 };

Same issues apply to all augmented assignment operators (+=)

docs.title: Lists
docs.text: The issue with order of assignment applies to struct lists as well.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: goboscript has a syntax inspired from languages such as C, Rust, and Python. Whitespace and indentation has no semantic significance. Statements end in semicolons.

docs.title: Comments
docs.text: # single-line comments are the only option.

docs.title: Numbers
docs.text:
0b111 # Binary:      7
0xFF  # Hexadecimal: 255
0o777 # Octal:       511
1024  # Decimal:     1024
3.141 # Float:       3.141

docs.title: Strings
docs.text:
"Hello, World!"
"Hello, \"World\"!"
"\u1234" # Unicode escape
"\n" # Newline
"\t" # Tab

docs.title: Booleans
docs.text:
true is replaced with 1 during compilation.

false is replaced with 0 during compilation.

docs.title: Boolean Coercion
docs.text:
Scratch does not allow placing a non-boolean block inside a boolean input. (With the exception of item # of list and item of list). goboscript will automatically coerce non-boolean blocks into conditions by wrapping them in a comparison with 1.

if timer() {
    ...
}

is equivalent to

if timer() == 1 {
    ...
}

docs.title: Declaration/Assignment
docs.text: There are two ways to declare a variable:

docs.title: 1. Declare using a top-level var statement.
docs.text: var variable_name = const_expr;

docs.title: 2. Declare by assigning a value to the variable.
docs.text:
The first assignment to a variable is considered its declaration.

variable_name = value;

type_name variable_name = value;

docs.title: Variables for all sprites
docs.text: If a variable is assigned to in stage.gs, it will be declared as for all sprites.

docs.title: Variables for this sprite only
docs.text: Variables are by-default declared as for this sprite only. If you want to declare a variable for all sprites, assign to it in stage.gs.

docs.title: Local Variables
docs.text:
Local variables are accessible only within the procedure they are declared in.

The first assignment with the local keyword will declare a local variable, all further uses of the variable will refer to the local variable. If a normal variable with the same name exists, it will be shadowed.

proc my_procedure {
    local x = 0;
    x = x + 1;
}

In the compiled Scratch project, the variable x will be named as my_procedure:x.

Note

Local variables will have undefined behavior if the procedure is recursive, or is NOT a run-without-screen-refresh procedure.

docs.title: Compound Assignment
docs.text: Operator Implementation x++; x--; x += y; x -= y; x *= y; x /= y; x //= y; x %= y; x &= y;

docs.title: Show/Hide Variable Monitor
docs.text:
show variable_name;

hide variable_name;
